children.name: Aegithina
children.description: A genus of small, active passerines found across South and Southeast Asia, with a slender build, short wings, and a habit of constant, restless movement through the canopy. Males are more brightly coloured than females, with bold black-and-yellow or green-and-yellow plumage patterns. They are highly vocal, with complex, whistled songs that are delivered in an energetic, undulating flight display.

children.name: Aegithina tiphia
children.description: A small, lively bird with a yellow-and-black breeding male, olive-green female, and a habit of performing acrobatic, undulating song-flights, widespread across South and Southeast Asia. It forages actively in the canopy for insects, often hanging upside-down from twigs. Its varied repertoire of whistles and trills makes it a familiar presence in gardens, parks, and forests.

children.name: Aegithina nigrolutea
children.description: A striking iora in which the breeding male is brilliant yellow with a black cap, back, wings, and tail, found in arid and semi-arid scrublands of the Indian subcontinent. The female is olive-yellow with grey wings. It inhabits dry thorn scrub and acacia woodlands, where it forages for insects in small, active parties.

children.name: Aegithina viridissima
children.description: A bright olive-green iora with a yellow belly and distinctive yellow wing-bars, found in lowland rainforests of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Unlike other ioras, it prefers the shady interior of primary and mature secondary forests. It is a restless forager that gleans insects from leaves and twigs in the mid-canopy.

children.name: Aegithina lafresnayei
children.description: The largest iora, with a male in bright yellow and black plumage and a distinctive white wing patch, found in forests from Myanmar through Indochina to the Malay Peninsula. It inhabits evergreen and deciduous forests, foraging deliberately in the canopy for insects. Its song is a clear, three-noted whistle.
